title: Fermi Surface Geometry from Charge Fluctuations in Three-Dimensional Metals
authors: Pok Man Tam, Yarden Sheffer, Xiao-Chuan Wu, F. D. M. Haldane, Shinsei Ryu
abstract: For three-dimensional non-interacting multi-band metals, we show that important information about the shape and the quantum geometry of Fermi surfaces is encoded in the subleading logarithmic term of bipartite charge fluctuations. This logarithmic term is related to the dimensionless $|\mathbf{q}|^3$-coefficient of the structure factor in momentum space, and both quantities can be expressed as Fermi surface integrals of the Fermi surface curvature tensor and the quantum metric tensor. When the real-space partition surface is a quadric (i.e., sphere or ellipsoid), the logarithmic coefficient satisfies a topological bound depending only on the Euler characteristic and the Chern number of the Fermi surface, illustrating a non-trivial interplay between topology and quantum topology in multi-band metals.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the shape and quantum geometry of 3D Fermi surfaces are imprinted on the subleading logarithmic scaling of charge fluctuations. It provides a mathematical link between physical observables and topological invariants like the Euler characteristic and Chern number. This establishes a way to probe the complex geometry of multi-band metals through density-density correlations.
main_problem: The paper investigates how the geometric and quantum geometric properties of 3D Fermi surfaces are encoded in the subleading logarithmic term of bipartite charge fluctuations.
main_result: The authors establish that the logarithmic coefficient of charge fluctuations can be decomposed into a geometric part (related to the Euler characteristic and Will and Willmore energy) and a quantum geometric part (related to the quantum metric and Chern number), and they derive a topological lower bound for this coefficient.
method: The study uses analytical expansions of the structure factor, differential geometry (curvature tensors, Gauss-Bonnet theorem), and the application of Wick's theorem and RPA to evaluate momentum-space integrals.
model_or_system: The system consists of three-dimensional non-interacting multi-band metals and Landau Fermi liquids, including specific models like the copper Fermi surface and Weyl metals.
key_observables: Bipartite charge fluctuations, structure factor S(q) (specifically the |q|^3 coefficient), and the logarithmic scaling coefficient Gamma.
important_parameters: Fermi surface curvature, Euler characteristic, Chern number, quantum metric tensor, Willmore energy, and the Landau parameter F0.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes zero temperature, the thermodynamic limit, non-interacting or Landau Fermi liquid regimes, and that different Fermi surface components are separated and do not intersect.
figures_summary: Figure 1 visualizes the structure factor overcounting and critical lines on the copper Fermi surface; Figure 3 shows the dependence of structure factor coefficients on the Landau parameter F0.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of encoding Fermi surface geometry in charge fluctuations, moves to the analytical derivation of the structure factor expansion, decomposes the logarithmic coefficient into geometric and quantum geometric parts, establishes topological bounds, and discusses implications for interacting Fermi liquids and Weyl systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Structure factor S(q) in Eq. (7) measures the volume of the green-shaded region between the Fermi surface (solid) and its shifted counterpart (dotted). S(1)(q) corresponds to the total shaded region, which overcounts a sliver of volume (red), S(3)(q), around Fermi surface critical points where q · ˆnF = 0. (b) Critical lines on the Fermi surface of copper are indicated (red) for a specific orien- tation of q. (c) ΓG as a function of EF for the model in Eq. (14), showing three topological classes of Fermi surface. Dashed lines in- dicate the corresponding topological lower bounds given by Eq. (13).

FIG. 2. Interplay between geometry and quantum geometry in a Weyl metal, see Eq. (19), with ΓG in (a) and ΓQG in (b), and Γ = ΓG + ΓQG in (c). Dashed lines indicate the topological lower bounds. (d) Illustration of Fermi surfaces, with the blue dots repre- senting Weyl points with ±-chirality.
FIG. 3. Coefficients of S(q) in isotropic Landau Fermi liquids, where F0 is the s-wave channel Landau parameter, S(3) 0 and S(1) 0 are the coefficients in a free Fermi gas (F0 = 0).
